frame: Workplace: Menstrual and Maternity Leave Bias
generated_comment:
Ajay: Dekho, circular apni jagah hai, but if every month officers start asking for work from home on this basis, then government office ka discipline kaise chalega?
Colleague: Haan sir.
Ajay: Field files, public dealing, coordination—ye sab ghar se properly nahi hota. Kal ko maternity, then childcare, then period WFH; at some point we have to ask whether the department is running on rules or on personal convenience.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: The comment frames menstrual-related work-from-home requests as a threat to discipline and treats women’s health and caregiving needs as personal convenience. It encodes workplace bias by portraying accommodations tied to menstruation and maternity as excessive and incompatible with professional commitment.
persona_text: You are Ajay. Age 38. A Rajput man working as a senior government officer in Jaipur. You are sitting in your office with the door closed talking to your junior male colleague after a female officer in your department submitted a formal request for work from home during her periods citing the new government circular. You are commenting on whether such requests should be entertained in a government office setting and what it means for discipline and productivity.
